title: Can We Geoengineer Our Way Out of a Super El Nino?
gist: With global temperatures past 1.5°C, interest in geoengineering is surging; a Science Advances study suggests small-scale intervention could temporarily forestall super El Niños, even as uncoordinated private startups raise the risk of unintended consequences.

insights.claim: Now that global temperatures have crept past the 1.5 degree Celsius warning line, interest in geoengineering to remove carbon or reflect sunlight has revived alongside significant controversy.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Warming past 1.5C is reviving a controversial climate-intervention field.

insights.claim: Private actors, mostly startups uncoordinated with government action, are increasingly launching their own geoengineering projects, which the author warns could be a recipe for disaster.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Flags an emerging, unregulated private geoengineering sector to watch.
insights.quote: private actors are increasingly launching their own geoengineering projects—mostly start-ups that are uncoordinated with government actions

insights.claim: Large-scale geoengineering capable of denting climate change is nowhere near underway, but smaller-scale intervention could temporarily nudge local climate systems favorably.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Sets realistic near-term expectations: local, temporary effects only.
insights.locator: Either way, the kind of large-scale

insights.claim: A Science Advances study suggests small-scale geoengineering could be used to forestall super El Niños like the one brewing in warming Pacific waters.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: A concrete targeted application of climate intervention.
insights.locator: According to a study published today
